data.title: Fresh Economic Instability Creates Fresh Challenges For S&P 500
data.description:
The S&P 500 index continues to slide as volatility in the Middle East spreads fear among investors. 

Over the past two weeks, as the Trump administration opted for a military intervention in Iran, sparking widespread disruption throughout the Middle East, the S&P 500 has shed more than 2.5% in value while oil prices continue to surge. 

According to one report conducted by fiscal analyst Kent Smetters, the war in Iran may ultimately cost the American economy up to $210 billion. 

The United States isn't alone in becoming adversely economically affected by the war, with global energy markets and gasoline prices sparking weakness across the S&P 500's global peers. 

To make matters more complicated, the US energy department has warned that petrol and diesel prices are unlikely to fall back to prewar levels until mid-2027 at the earliest, prompting more fears over the prospect of inflation creeping back into the economy. 

Official figures now show that US petrol prices have climbed 19% over the past two weeks to $3.50 a gallon, while energy supplies have been throttled due to disruptions in the Strait of Hormuz. Likewise, diesel prices have increased 28% to $4.86 a gallon. 

Forecasts from the Energy Information Administration suggest that petrol won't drop back below its pre-conflict level of $2.94 until the end of 2027, while it predicts that diesel won't return to $3.81 until the middle of next year. 

Given the invaluable role that diesel plays in supporting US industry, prolonged price hikes could lead to long-term economic pressures that may hinder the performance of domestic markets throughout the year ahead. 

Mounting Economic Challenges

The timing of the conflict in Iran comes as the US economy is contending with mounting contractions in the domestic labor market. 

Payrolls throughout the United States fell by 92,000 in February as unemployment rose to 4.4%, according to official figures. Worryingly, this downturn has bewildered analysts, who had largely expected hiring to remain stable. 

Instead, the economy has been left to come to terms with its biggest monthly job loss since the government shut down in October. 

The figures have added fuel to concerns about the rise in oil prices in recent days, and almost every domestic sector shed jobs, with losses even experienced in the typically resilient healthcare sector. 

Federal government employment also shrank by 10,000 in February, with total jobs dropping by 330,000, or 11%, since reaching a peak in October 2024. 

Could the S&P 500 Fall Further?

Given that the S&P 500 has experienced such exponential growth in recent years following the start of the artificial intelligence boom on Wall Street, the prospect of fresh economic vulnerability in the United States has been particularly concerning for investors. 

The S&P 500's growth in recent years has led to sky-high valuations that could be more prone to economic pressures as they emerge. 

The ongoing geopolitical concerns sparked by oil price increases have a precedent in the 2022 Russian invasion of Ukraine, which saw a peak of $139 per barrel in the US. 

However, there are some important distinctions to make between 2026 and 2022. The Fed had the room to act decisively in hiking rates to cool inflation, which itself had been accelerated by a series of stimulus packages in the pandemic. Given that the S&P 500 surged 26% one year later in 2023 off the back of the emerging AI boom, it's clear that even deep declines can prompt fast recoveries over time. 

Looking Overseas for Resilience

Many global markets have been rocked by the escalations in the Middle East, and even China's famously resilient Hang Seng index has struggled to maintain its momentum as headwinds grow. 

March saw China set its lowest annual growth target on record, which was dropped from 5% to 4.5% off the back of tariff pressures and issues linked to deflation taking hold. 

Hong Kong's Hang Seng index fell 1.35% in the wake of the conflict in Iran, but its relative strength could still inspire more investors to look to Asia for stronger recoveries than the S&P 500. 

Thanks to a strong 2025, which saw the Hang Seng grow 30%, it's clear that China has plenty of precedent when it comes to rebounding from setbacks like tariff pressures from the United States. 

Innovations in AI have helped to support more domestic growth, while the Chinese government has implemented fresh stimulus measures in a bid to support the nation's economic recovery. 

As a result, China is benefiting from more frictionless market entry opportunities than ever before, in a boost to domestic production and supply chain resilience. 

Should the Hang Seng post a stronger recovery than the S&P 500 as the fallout from the Middle East continues to be determined, we could see more investors look to Asia as an alternative to the high valuations in the US. 

What's Next for the S&P 500?

The S&P 500's growth is becoming increasingly tied to the overall economic health of the United States, and at a time when the fallout of the conflict in Iran and unexpected job market movements are sparking fresh fears over the state of the economy in 2026, the coming weeks may be pivotal in learning the direction that the index will take over the year ahead. 

De-escalation in the Middle East can assist the Federal Reserve in maintaining a relatively loose monetary policy, while any signs of a reversal in unemployment rates will help to restore some investor confidence in US markets. 

However, with petrol and diesel prices set to remain higher for longer, we may see more investors adopt a global outlook for their portfolios as a means of balancing risk in 2026 and beyond.

Disclosure: On the date of publication, Dmytro Spilka did not hold (either directly or indirectly) any positions in the securities mentioned in this article. The opinions expressed in this article are those of the writer. Dmytro Spilka does not intend to make a trade in any of the securities mentioned above in the next 72 hours.

data.title: Digital Realty's Dividend Looks Stable. The Capital Cycle Deserves More Attention.
data.description:
Record bookings, 8% FFO growth guidance — and a $10 billion construction pipeline that runs on a different clock than the dividend.

Stability

Digital Realty Trust (NYSE:DLR) closed 2025 with its strongest leasing year on record. Core FFO came in at $7.39 per share for the full year — up 10% over 2024 and above guidance. The company signed over $1.2 billion in new leases, nearly 70% above its prior five-year average, and entered 2026 with a record backlog of approximately $1.4 billion on a total basis, or roughly $817 million at DLR’s share.

The dividend stands at $4.88 per share annually. Against 2026 Core FFO guidance of $7.90–$8.00 per share, that produces a buffer of roughly 38% at the midpoint — meaning the company retains approximately 38 cents of every dollar earned after paying the dividend. For a data center REIT operating at this scale, that cushion is meaningful.

The balance sheet reflects BBB/Baa2 credit ratings across S&P and Moody’s. Net debt to Adjusted EBITDA stood at 4.9x at year-end 2025 — below the 5x threshold that often draws attention from credit analysts. Total debt of approximately $18.4 billion is spread across a weighted average maturity of roughly five years, with no single near-term concentration that would force a disruptive refinancing event.

On the surface, the income picture looks relatively straightforward: a growing dividend, a widening buffer, and a balance sheet that is not under near-term pressure.

Cautious

The complication with Digital Realty is not the dividend. The pressure sits in the capital cycle that has to fund growth before that growth becomes cash flow.

In DLR’s case, that capital cycle is the gap between signing demand, securing power, funding construction, and waiting for that capacity to turn into rent.

DLR currently has approximately 769 megawatts under construction within a gross development pipeline of roughly $10 billion. That pipeline is being funded through a combination of debt issuance, asset dispositions, an equity ATM program, and a newly established private capital fund — into which the company contributed 80% stakes in stabilized seed assets, generating approximately $427 million in net proceeds in late 2025.

This is not a traditional net lease REIT model. The key risk is not just refinancing existing debt at a higher coupon. It is having to keep funding capacity before that capacity begins producing rent.

In a net lease REIT, dividend stress typically emerges when rent coverage weakens or refinancing costs compress retained cash flow. In a data center REIT, the timing mismatch is different. Capital often has to be committed well before revenue begins, which means execution risk sits earlier in the cycle — and further from the dividend itself.

Power availability has emerged as the primary operational constraint. Management noted that supply is now largely power-driven, and the company is deploying a 5-gigawatt power bank to secure capacity ahead of demand. That strategy requires capital commitment before revenue materializes — which is the structural dynamic that separates data center REITs from their net lease peers.

The 2026 guidance of $7.90–$8.00 Core FFO per share is stated on a constant currency basis — meaning the reported figure could diverge from guidance without any change in underlying business performance. DLR operates across North America, Europe, and Asia-Pacific, and currency movements represent the most likely source of noise between operational execution and reported results.

What Would Shift

What I’d Watch

Digital Realty’s current dividend does not appear to be under structural pressure. The more important question is whether the capital cycle remains supportive enough to keep future earnings growth on schedule — and whether backlog, power, and construction timing continue to line up.

This is not a prediction — structural assessment only.

Benzinga Disclaimer: This article is from an unpaid external contributor. It does not represent Benzinga’s reporting and has not been edited for content or accuracy.

data.title: Carnival (CCL) Stock Slides As Middle East Tensions Lift Oil
data.description:
Carnival Corp (NYSE:CCL) shares are trading lower Thursday morning as investors react to a sharp escalation in Middle East-related energy risk, a development that matters deeply for the world's largest cruise company. Here’s what investors need to know.

  • Carnival stock is feeling bearish pressure. What’s behind CCL decline?

U.S.-Iran Conflict Escalates As Oil Supply Risks Rise

Fresh reports said the U.S.-Iran conflict has intensified, with the United Nations Security Council condemning Iranian attacks and U.S. officials moving to release 172 million barrels from the Strategic Petroleum Reserve to help contain energy costs.

The bigger issue for Carnival is oil. A separate report said Goldman Sachs now views the disruption near the Strait of Hormuz as the largest oil supply shock on record, with Persian Gulf exports falling to roughly 3% of normal levels.

Goldman raised its Brent crude forecast to an average of $98 in March and April, while warning prices could climb even higher if the disruption persists. U.S. oil prices were already up about 6% Thursday morning.

Rising Oil Prices Threaten Carnival's Operating Costs And Margins

That is especially important for Carnival because fuel is one of the company's biggest operating costs. As a global cruise operator, Carnival depends on affordable bunker fuel to run its fleet and protect margins.

When oil spikes, investors worry that higher fuel expense could pressure profitability, especially if the company cannot fully offset those costs through ticket prices, onboard spending, or hedging.

The news also raises broader macro concerns. Goldman said higher oil could lift inflation, slow GDP growth and delay Federal Reserve rate cuts, a mix that could weaken consumer travel demand.

CCL Shares Slide Thursday Morning

CCL Price Action: Carnival shares were down 6.12% at $24.38 at the time of publication on Thursday, according to Benzinga Pro data.

Image: Shutterstock

data.title: Jay Chou-Powered Star Plus Legend Swings To A Loss In 2025
data.description:
The company with strong ties to the Taiwanese Mandopop king swung into the red last year, blaming slower content launches and impaired trade receivables

Key Takeaways:

  • Star Plus Legend said it lost up to 52 million yuan last year, reversing a profit of 50.2 million yuan in 2024
  • The company, closely tied to pop star Jay Chou, reported declining revenues for its IP business in the first half of last year, but has been aggressively diversifying since then

Taiwanese singer and songwriter Jay Chou has sold more than 30 million records and is regularly likened to an Asian edition of Taylor Swift, with 16 spiders and an asteroid named after him and his songs. He was the first Asian singer to top the global album charts with his 15th studio album, Greatest Works of Art, in 2022. But at 47, the "King of Mandopop" isn't getting any younger.

That may be a problem for Star Plus Legend Holdings Ltd. (6683.HK), which is closely tied to the superstar, and last week warned that it lost up to 52 million yuan ($7.5 million) last year, marking a sharp reversal from its 50.2 million profit in 2024.

Star Plus blamed the reversal of fortune on restructuring as well as a slower pace of content launches and impairment provisions on trade receivables – showing some of its clients aren't paying their bills in China's slowing economy. "The group is currently at a critical stage of upgrading its business structure and strategy," it said.

Perhaps many of the company's investors are Jay Chou fans, which might explain why they shrugged off the news and rewarded the stock with a 13% rise over the week after the announcement, taking it to a four-month high.

Star Plus makes money from a coffee brand endorsed by Chou and licensing rights to his cartoon alter ego, Chou Chou. A social media influencer, Liu Genghong, notorious for wearing a tight-fitting Aquaman costume on his livestreaming fitness program, is another Star Plus frontman, but Chou is by far the company's superstar when it comes to its revenue makeup.

Even so, Star Plus Legend's results from the first half of last year show the Jay Chou brand may be aging. The company's revenue rose 33% year-on-year to 354.5 million yuan, largely thanks to its new retail business, whose revenue nearly doubled to 210.7 million yuan from 110 million yuan a year earlier. But revenue from its IP segment sagged by 8.2% to 144 million yuan.

The IP slowdown was a reversal from a much stronger 2024, when revenue from IP and content creation grew by 65.1%, raising questions about the company's overwhelming reliance on the shaggy-haired Chou.

Chou set up Star Plus in 2017 with Yang Chun-Jang, his manager, Yeh Hui-mei, his mother, and Chen Zhong, one of his artistic managers. Although Chou has no direct shareholding in the company, he made it an investor and production agent for his own company, JVR Music, which is also owned 5% by his mother. Chou owns 40% of JVR and Yang owns 45%.  

New initiatives

New retail, one of Star Plus' two main business lines, mainly consists of a low-carbohydrate coffee business under the company's Modong brand, but also includes skincare products and a separate line of matcha powder. Its IP business includes event management and content creation, which both rely heavily on Chou's star power.

At the time of its 2023 Hong Kong IPO, the company's prospectus noted the risk of over-dependence on Chou's popularity. In 2019, his "promotional effect" accounted for 83% of the company's new retail segment revenue, gradually declining to 45.2% by 2022. Revenue from content creation centered on Chou grew from 5.7% to 13.3% of the total over that time.

Huayuan projected the company's revenue would grow 36.2% last year to 795 million yuan, and forecast even faster growth through 2027. "The company is transforming from a traditional ‘IP creator and operator' to a ‘communicator of happiness' through a series of strategic deployments," it said, rating the stock as "overweight."

More recently, Star Plus has signed deals with other high-profile Korean artists, including Kim Joong-kook and Song Kangpho. It is also considering a U.S. IPO on the Nasdaq according to Korean media reports.

Last September the company also purchased 1.17% of the shares in Beijing's Bird Nest National Stadium, becoming the only private shareholder in the central venue for the Beijing 2008 Olympics. And it is partnering with robotic sensation Unitree, a creator of the crowd-pleasing robots from China's last two "Spring Festival Gala" TV programs, in a joint venture to develop character-based robots, starting with a robot dog.

How is it paying for its new series of deals? Last November, the company announced two new stock issues involving about 130 million new shares, raising a combined HK$760 million ($97 million). Those followed another sale in August when the company raised HK$324 million through the sale of 37.5 million shares.

All this shows that Star Plus is thinking about the next steps as Jay Chou's star inevitably fades, and has built up its war chest to pay for its new ventures. Now, investors will be waiting to see if some of those new initiatives can bring back some of the star power to Star Plus' business.

To subscribe to Bamboo Works weekly free newsletter, click here

data.title: Fertilizer Stocks Rise As Strait Of Hormuz Shipping Disruption Threatens Global Supply
data.description:
Shares of fertilizer stocks are trading higher Thursday amid concern that the ongoing closure of the Strait of Hormuz will limit exports from key Middle East producers Saudi Arabia and the UAE.

Fertilizer Stocks Gain As Strait Of Hormuz Disruption Threatens Supply

According to Business Insider, conflict in the Middle East has disrupted shipping through the Strait of Hormuz, a critical route for global fertilizer trade, driving a rally in fertilizer-related stocks. 

The waterway is a key transit point for fertilizer products and materials such as urea, ammonia, sulfur and nitrogen used in global agriculture. Analysts warn that disruptions to shipments through the strait could significantly impact fertilizer supply and prices worldwide. 

The Middle East accounts for a substantial share of global fertilizer exports, with roughly 25% to 35% of fertilizer trade passing through the Strait of Hormuz. 

As exports from producers in the region face potential constraints, fertilizer prices have risen, lifting shares of companies tied to the sector.

Fertilizer Stocks Climb

Price Action: At the time of publication, CF shares are trading 7.30% higher at $128.90, Nutrien shares are trading 6.72% higher at $84.64, and Mosaic shares are 4.67% higher at $30.51, according to data from Benzinga Pro.

Image via Shutterstock
